text:
HEMATOLOGY / MEDICAL ONCOLOGY
OUTPATIENT PROGRESS NOTE — GASTROINTESTINAL ONCOLOGY
With Palliative Care Co-Management

ATTENDING PHYSICIAN: [Attending Oncologist]
PALLIATIVE CARE CO-MANAGEMENT: [Palliative Care Attending]
PATIENT: Marcus T. Delgado
DOB: 1965
MRN: [MRN on file]

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT:
Return visit for ongoing management of metastatic colorectal cancer; disease assessment following third-line therapy; palliative care co-management visit for symptom assessment and goals-of-care discussion.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS:

Mr. Delgado is a 59-year-old Hispanic male with a known diagnosis of Stage IV (AJCC 8th edition) KRAS G12D-mutated, MSS adenocarcinoma of the sigmoid colon with synchronous bilobar hepatic metastases, now with additional pulmonary metastatic disease. He presents today for a combined medical oncology and palliative care visit following his most recent CT restaging scan, which demonstrated marginal disease stability (borderline stable disease) on his current third-line regimen of TAS-102 (Lonsurf) plus bevacizumab.

To briefly summarize his oncologic course to date: Mr. Delgado was initially diagnosed approximately 21 months ago with Stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma. His tumor harbors a KRAS p.G12D mutation (VAF 42%), which precludes use of anti-EGFR therapy (cetuximab or panitumumab). Additional molecular features include TP53 p.R248W, APC p.R1450*, SMAD4 p.R361H, PIK3CA p.E545K, MYC amplification, and MSS status with low TMB (3.2 mut/Mb). He completed 12 cycles of first-line FOLFOX plus bevacizumab, achieving a partial response (nadir CEA 87 ng/mL, best hepatic response with segment VI lesion shrinking from 3.8 cm to 2.3 cm), before transitioning to maintenance 5-FU/leucovorin/bevacizumab per the OPTIMOX1-informed strategy. Oxaliplatin was discontinued at cycle 10 due to grade 3 peripheral sensory neuropathy with cumulative dose of approximately 760 mg/m2. He progressed at approximately 10 months with new bilateral pulmonary nodules, and moved to second-line FOLFIRI plus ramucirumab (per RAISE trial). He achieved disease stabilization on second-line therapy for approximately 7 months before again progressing with an expanding pulmonary burden and new hepatic deposits. He transitioned to third-line TAS-102/Lonsurf plus bevacizumab per the SUNLIGHT regimen (dose adjusted to 30 mg/m2 after a cycle 2 episode of grade 3 neutropenia requiring G-CSF and dose reduction). His most recent CT scan (performed approximately 2 weeks ago, after 3 cycles of TAS-102/bevacizumab) showed borderline stable disease — the segment VI hepatic lesion has crept up slightly to 3.1 cm and the segment V lesion is 0.9 cm; right lower lobe lung nodule now 1.2 cm. CEA at that imaging visit was 312 ng/mL, continuing to trend upward, and CA 19-9 was 245 U/mL.

At today's visit, we are addressing his functional status, which has notably declined since the prior visit. He and his wife Claudia are present, along with his adult daughter who traveled specifically for this appointment. He reports marked increase in fatigue over the past 6-8 weeks. He describes it as a "bone-deep exhaustion" that limits his ability to perform usual activities — he can no longer manage the household tasks he was doing previously, and has not been able to return to even part-time work as a teacher for the past several weeks. He spends the majority of his day either resting in a recliner or in bed. He notes decreased appetite for approximately 6 weeks — he states he is eating perhaps 30-40% of his normal meal volumes, and has lost approximately 8 pounds over the past 2 months (weight today 168 lbs vs. 176 lbs two months ago). He describes early satiety — "I feel full after just a few bites." He also notes worsening abdominal discomfort, described as a dull, aching pressure in the right upper quadrant and epigastric area, rated 3-4/10 at baseline, occasionally peaking to 5-6/10 after eating. He takes acetaminophen 1000 mg TID at home with partial relief.

He denies frank nausea but endorses mild queasiness after eating. He denies frank vomiting. He denies dyspnea at rest but notes mild shortness of breath with exertion (walking to the bathroom, negotiating stairs). He denies hemoptysis, hematemesis, melena, or hematochezia. No reported fevers or chills. He has mild dependent edema in the bilateral ankles, which his wife notes is new over the past 3 weeks.

His peripheral neuropathy, which peaked at grade 3 during oxaliplatin treatment, has been gradually improving and is now grade 1 — occasional intermittent tingling in the feet, no longer significantly limiting. He was tapered off gabapentin over the past several weeks with minimal worsening.

Hypertension has been managed on a triple antihypertensive regimen (amlodipine 10 mg, lisinopril 20 mg, hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg) with generally adequate control, though he has had occasional readings in the 150s systolic at home.

His type 2 diabetes remains relatively well controlled on metformin 1000 mg BID, though reduced oral intake has naturally led to lower glucose readings.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS:

Constitutional: Significant fatigue and malaise (see HPI). Weight loss 8 lbs over 2 months. Decreased appetite, anorexia. No fever, night sweats.
Head/eyes/ears/nose/throat: No headaches. No visual changes. No epistaxis.
Cardiovascular: Mild bilateral ankle edema (new). No chest pain. No palpitations. Mild exertional dyspnea.
Pulmonary: Mild exertional dyspnea as noted. No cough, no hemoptysis, no pleuritic chest pain.
Gastrointestinal: Decreased appetite, early satiety, dull right upper quadrant and epigastric discomfort (3-4/10). Mild queasiness. No frank nausea or vomiting. Bowel movements approximately once every 2 days, formed — constipation manageable with increased hydration. No blood in stool. No jaundice noted by patient.
Genitourinary: No hematuria. Reduced urine output in setting of decreased intake. No dysuria.
Musculoskeletal: Generalized weakness. Residual mild neuropathic tingling in feet (grade 1). No new bone pain.
Neurological: No focal neurological deficits. Fatigue-related cognitive slowing reported by patient ("brain fog"). No headache or vision changes to suggest CNS involvement.
Psychiatric: Patient endorses sadness and emotional heaviness related to disease trajectory. He and family are processing what this illness means going forward. No active suicidal ideation. Anxiety present around disease burden and family impact.
Endocrine/metabolic: Diabetes as noted, currently well managed given reduced oral intake.
Skin: No new rashes or lesions.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY (summarized, see prior notes for full detail):
1. Stage IV sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma (KRAS G12D-mutant, MSS), with bilobar hepatic and bilateral pulmonary metastases — current active problem
2. Type 2 diabetes mellitus — diagnosed approximately 10 years ago, managed on metformin
3. Hypertension — pre-existing (mild at baseline), significantly worsened during bevacizumab and ramucirumab therapy; currently managed on triple therapy
4. Oxaliplatin-induced peripheral sensory neuropathy — peak grade 3, currently grade 1 in recovery
5. Former smoking history — approximately 30 pack-years, quit 12 years prior

SURGICAL HISTORY:
No prior abdominal or oncologic surgeries.

FAMILY HISTORY:
Father: colorectal cancer (died in his 60s). Paternal germline testing discussed previously; no pathogenic variants identified in patient's own germline (APC, MLH1/MSH2/MSH6/PMS2, MUTYH all negative on NGS).

SOCIAL HISTORY:
High school mathematics teacher; currently on extended medical leave. Married to Claudia (37 years). Two adult children — daughter (present today) and son. Lives at home with spouse; daughter is now helping on weekends. Non-drinker. Former smoker (quit 12 years ago, approximately 30 pack-years). No illicit drug use. Lives in a single-story home, which is favorable for ambulatory limitations. Has maintained strong social and spiritual support network through his church community.

ALLERGIES:
No known drug allergies.

MEDICATIONS (current):
1. TAS-102 (trifluridine/tipiracil, Lonsurf) 30 mg/m2 orally BID with food, days 1-5 and 8-12 of each 28-day cycle (dose-reduced from 35 mg/m2 after cycle 2 neutropenia)
2. Bevacizumab (Avastin) 5 mg/kg IV every 2 weeks (days 1 and 15 of each cycle) — next infusion scheduled
3. Amlodipine 10 mg orally daily
4. Lisinopril 20 mg orally daily
5. Hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg orally daily
6. Metformin 1000 mg orally BID (with meals)
7. Ondansetron 8 mg orally PRN nausea (dispensed)
8. Acetaminophen 1000 mg orally TID (for abdominal discomfort)
9. Docusate sodium 100 mg orally BID (constipation prophylaxis)
10. Bisacodyl 10 mg PRN (constipation rescue)
11. Prochlorperazine 10 mg orally TID PRN nausea (prescribed at last visit per palliative care)
12. Methylphenidate (Ritalin) 5 mg orally every morning (fatigue — prescribed last visit)

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION:

General: Mr. Delgado is a thin, visibly fatigued Hispanic male who appears older than his stated age. He is sitting in the exam chair, reclining slightly with his wife at his side. He is alert and oriented x3, conversant, though his affect is subdued. He is in no acute distress but looks tired and emotionally burdened.

Vital Signs:
- Blood pressure: 148/90 mmHg (slightly elevated; right arm, seated)
- Heart rate: 88 bpm, regular
- Respiratory rate: 18 breaths/min
- Temperature: 36.8°C (97.8°F)
- SpO2: 95% on room air (baseline; was 98% approximately 6 months ago)
- Weight: 168 lbs (76.2 kg) — down 8 lbs (3.6 kg) from 2 months ago
- Height: 5'10" (177.8 cm)
- BMI: 24.0 kg/m2
- ECOG Performance Status: 2-3 — notable decline from PS 1 at prior visit; patient is now spending more than 50% of daytime hours in a chair or bed; self-care largely preserved but limited beyond basic activities of daily living

Head, Eyes, Ears, Nose, Throat: Normocephalic. Scleral icterus absent. Conjunctivae mildly pale. Mucous membranes moist. No oral mucositis. No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy palpated.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. No murmurs. Mild bilateral pitting ankle edema, 1+, to the lower shin.

Pulmonary: Respirations unlabored at rest. Breath sounds clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rales, or rhonchi appreciated. No dullness to percussion at lung bases (no detectable pleural effusion on exam today).

Abdomen: Soft. Mild distension in the lower abdomen without gross tympany to suggest large-volume ascites at this time; however, there is subtle fullness in the flanks bilaterally with a positive fluid wave on careful assessment — consistent with small-volume ascites noted on recent imaging. Liver edge palpated approximately 3 cm below the right costal margin — firm, slightly tender to deep palpation in the right upper quadrant. Spleen not distinctly palpable. No masses palpated in the left lower quadrant at the primary tumor site. Bowel sounds present and normoactive. No peritoneal signs.

Extremities: Bilateral ankle pitting edema 1+ as noted. Residual mild muscle wasting in the lower extremities. No signs of DVT.

Neurological: Cranial nerves grossly intact. Motor strength preserved in upper and lower extremities at 4+/5 globally in the context of fatigue and deconditioning. Sensation mildly decreased in bilateral feet to mid-calf — consistent with residual grade 1 neuropathy. Gait steady but slow; patient uses the wall for support on one occasion during ambulation across the room. No cerebellar signs.

Skin: No jaundice. Mild cachexia evident. No new skin lesions or rashes.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS (today and recent):

Complete Blood Count (today):
- WBC: 3.8 x10^9/L (low-normal; nadir trend post-TAS-102)
- Hemoglobin: 10.2 g/dL (low; mild anemia — new compared to 11.1 g/dL 4 weeks ago)
- Hematocrit: 30.8%
- Platelets: 172 x10^9/L
- ANC: 2.1 x10^9/L (adequate; above neutropenia threshold)

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel (today):
- Sodium: 134 mEq/L (mildly low; consistent with poor po intake and early hyponatremia)
- Potassium: 3.9 mEq/L
- Chloride: 99 mEq/L
- Bicarbonate: 23 mEq/L
- BUN: 24 mg/dL
- Creatinine: 1.1 mg/dL (stable; eGFR ~65)
- Glucose: 98 mg/dL (well controlled given reduced intake)
- Total bilirubin: 1.4 mg/dL (mildly elevated; upper limit of normal; compare to 0.8 mg/dL 3 months ago)
- Direct bilirubin: 0.6 mg/dL (mildly elevated)
- AST: 67 U/L (elevated; up from 44 U/L prior)
- ALT: 54 U/L (elevated; up from 38 U/L prior)
- Alkaline phosphatase: 218 U/L (elevated; up from 142 U/L prior; hepatic metastatic burden)
- Albumin: 2.9 g/dL (low; hypoalbuminemia, nutritional and oncologic)
- Total protein: 5.8 g/dL
- LDH: 312 U/L (elevated, consistent with progressive disease burden)

Tumor Markers (from imaging visit ~2 weeks ago):
- CEA: 312 ng/mL (up from 287 ng/mL at progression, and from 79 ng/mL at nadir on maintenance)
- CA 19-9: 245 U/mL (rising trend)

Coagulation (today):
- PT/INR: 1.2 (normal)
- PTT: 30 seconds (normal)

Urinalysis: No proteinuria (spot urine protein:creatinine 0.18 — within normal limits for bevacizumab monitoring).

HbA1c (most recent, 6 weeks ago): 6.4% — well controlled.

---

IMAGING RESULTS (most recent CT CAP, performed approximately 2 weeks prior to today's visit):

As reviewed at today's visit and discussed with the patient and family:

The CT showed borderline stability of known disease — the liver metastases are minimally changed (segment VI lesion now 3.1 cm, segment V lesion 0.9 cm), the multiple bilateral pulmonary nodules are essentially unchanged, and the primary sigmoid mass is stable at approximately 3.1 cm. There was no dramatic new progression on this scan, though the trajectory of the CEA marker (rising to 312 ng/mL) is concerning and out of proportion to the imaging stability, which may suggest early progression not yet fully apparent on CT morphology. Small-volume ascites is now noted on the scan, a new finding that represents peritoneal involvement — a concerning development that indicates the disease has extended beyond the hepatic and pulmonary compartments.

Prior imaging summary for context:
- Baseline (diagnosis): 4 hepatic lesions (sum 8.1 cm target lesion measurement), no pulmonary disease
- Post 12-cycle FOLFOX/bevacizumab (best response): 4 hepatic lesions (sum 4.6 cm), no pulmonary disease
- Progression on maintenance (month 10): new bilateral pulmonary nodules, hepatic lesions minimally growing
- Post 4 cycles FOLFIRI/ramucirumab: stabilization of all known disease
- Progression on FOLFIRI/ramucirumab (month 17): new lung nodules (now 6 bilateral), new hepatic segment V lesion, rising markers
- Post 3 cycles TAS-102/bevacizumab (current): borderline stable, new small ascites, rising CEA

---

ASSESSMENT:

1. Metastatic colorectal adenocarcinoma (KRAS G12D-mutant, MSS, Stage IV), now with hepatic, bilateral pulmonary, and peritoneal (ascites) metastatic disease — currently on third-line TAS-102 plus bevacizumab (SUNLIGHT regimen, cycle 4 in planning), with borderline stable disease on most recent imaging but rising tumor markers and worsening functional status.

2. Functional decline — ECOG PS 2-3, representing a meaningful and concerning deterioration from ECOG PS 1 at the start of third-line therapy. Patient is spending more than 50% of daytime hours at rest. This decline is multifactorial: progressive disease burden, treatment-related fatigue from TAS-102, mild anemia, nutritional decline, and psychosocial/emotional stress.

3. Cancer-associated anorexia-cachexia — weight loss of 8 lbs over 2 months, significant reduction in oral intake (estimated 30-40% of usual), hypoalbuminemia (2.9 g/dL), early satiety. Contributing to functional decline and reduced treatment tolerance.

4. New small-volume ascites — peritoneal extension of disease, likely malignant in etiology given clinical context. Currently low volume, not requiring therapeutic intervention but requires monitoring.

5. Worsening liver function — rising alkaline phosphatase, AST/ALT, and mildly elevated total bilirubin compared to prior, consistent with increasing hepatic tumor burden. Functional hepatic reserve at present appears adequate but this trajectory is worrisome.

6. Hypertension — on triple antihypertensive therapy; blood pressure 148/90 today; slightly sub-optimally controlled; anti-VEGF effect persists with ongoing bevacizumab.

7. Mild anemia — hemoglobin 10.2 g/dL, likely multifactorial (TAS-102-related myelosuppression, chronic disease/inflammation). Does not meet threshold for transfusion currently; monitor.

8. Residual grade 1 peripheral sensory neuropathy — recovering, intermittent, not a primary limitation at this time.

9. Type 2 diabetes mellitus — well controlled, no active concern.

10. Psychosocial distress — patient and family processing difficult clinical trajectory; existential anxiety, appropriate grief response. Family actively engaged and present.

---

PLAN:

ONCOLOGIC MANAGEMENT:
This is a pivotal and difficult visit. Mr. Delgado has received three lines of systemic therapy over the course of approximately 21 months. His current regimen (TAS-102/bevacizumab) is showing marginal, likely tenuous, disease control — imaging is borderline stable but markers are rising, functional status has declined meaningfully, and new ascites signals peritoneal involvement. We have had an extended, candid, and compassionate discussion today with Mr. Delgado, his wife Claudia, and his adult daughter regarding the trajectory of his illness.

We discussed:
- The current disease status in accessible, non-technical language: "The scan from two weeks ago showed the cancer is not dramatically worse, but it's also not responding — it's essentially holding its ground at best, while you are feeling significantly worse. The blood marker has continued to climb, and we've seen a small amount of new fluid in the abdomen, which tells us the cancer has spread to the lining of the belly — a new site we haven't seen before."
- The expected prognosis honestly and gently: this is an advanced, treatment-refractory malignancy. Given his trajectory through three lines of therapy, declining PS, rising markers, peritoneal extension, and worsening hepatic function, the median prognosis at this juncture is measured in months.
- Fourth-line therapy options: regorafenib (which he did not previously receive) and combination therapies. However, in the context of ECOG PS 2-3, declining hepatic reserve, and worsening functional status, the likelihood of meaningful clinical benefit from additional cytotoxic therapy is low, and the risk of treatment-related harm is high. This was discussed openly.
- Clinical trial options: KRAS G12D-targeted therapies (e.g., MRTX1133 and related agents in development) remain investigational and not available outside of clinical trials. We discussed that while he had previously declined trial enrollment, the option remains open if he wishes to pursue this, and we will facilitate referral to a major academic center if desired. However, enrollment eligibility will depend on PS criteria and his ability to travel and tolerate protocol requirements.

After extensive conversation, Mr. Delgado stated clearly and repeatedly, with full understanding of the implications: "I want to keep fighting, but I don't want to feel worse than I already do. I want to be home with my family. If the treatment is going to take away the time I have left, I don't want it." His wife and daughter affirmed his wishes.

Decision regarding TAS-102/bevacizumab: We will continue the current regimen for now at the dose-reduced TAS-102 level (30 mg/m2), given that the imaging showed marginal stability. We acknowledge this is a time-limited trial of continued therapy. If the patient's functional status worsens further, or if the next imaging demonstrates clear progression, we will discontinue all cytotoxic therapy and transition fully to comfort-focused hospice care. The patient and family agree with and endorse this plan explicitly.

Bevacizumab — given the new ascites and peritoneal involvement, we will continue bevacizumab as part of the current regimen for its anti-VEGF effect on both the metastatic disease and potentially the ascites. We will reassess at next visit.

Next CT restaging: planned in approximately 6-8 weeks. If disease has clearly worsened (particularly hepatic or peritoneal burden), we will have an immediate goals-of-care reassessment.

SYMPTOM MANAGEMENT (Palliative Care co-plan):

Fatigue:
- Continue methylphenidate 5 mg each morning; consider uptitrating to 10 mg if tolerated and helpful
- Encourage light activity as tolerated (short walks within the home)
- Address anemia — see below

Anemia:
- Hemoglobin 10.2 g/dL; not transfusing today. If Hgb drops below 8.0 g/dL or patient becomes significantly symptomatic (worsening dyspnea, dizziness), will transfuse 1-2 units pRBC
- Check iron studies and B12/folate at next visit (nutritional deficiencies as contributing factor)

Anorexia / Cachexia:
- Clinical dietitian referral placed (formal nutrition assessment and high-calorie supplementation counseling)
- Consider megestrol acetate 400-800 mg daily for appetite stimulation — discussed risk/benefit (DVT risk, fluid retention); will discuss with patient and initiate at next visit if Claudia and patient agree
- Continue prochlorperazine TID PRN for nausea management
- Ensure adequate access to preferred foods; counsel family on small frequent meals, calorie-dense foods, nutritional supplements (Ensure, Boost)

Abdominal Pain:
- Acetaminophen 1000 mg TID for baseline RUQ/epigastric discomfort — adequate at this level
- Prescribe tramadol 50 mg orally q6h PRN for breakthrough pain if acetaminophen is insufficient (titrate to lowest effective dose; mindful of constipation and sedation in setting of methylphenidate use)
- If pain escalates, low-dose opioid (oral morphine IR) will be initiated — discuss timing with patient and family

Ascites:
- Small volume at present; no therapeutic paracentesis indicated today
- Recommend mild sodium restriction (2 g/day diet); discontinue HCTZ given hypoalbuminemia and risk of prerenal azotemia
- Begin spironolactone 50 mg daily for ascites management; adjust as tolerated
- Monitor weight daily at home; if weight increases >5 lbs over 1 week or abdomen becomes significantly more distended, call the office
- If ascites becomes symptomatic (significant distension, dyspnea, discomfort), plan for therapeutic paracentesis through interventional radiology or outpatient procedure suite

Hypertension:
- Remove HCTZ (see above — replacing with spironolactone will have some antihypertensive effect and addresses ascites)
- Continue amlodipine 10 mg and lisinopril 20 mg
- BP target in this setting is pragmatic (<150/90 mmHg), not aggressive, given overall clinical context

Constipation:
- Increase docusate to twice daily; add senna 2 tabs BID for ongoing bowel regimen
- Given anticipated tramadol initiation, bowel regimen will be escalated proactively

Anxiety / Psychosocial Distress:
- Continue palliative care involvement — follow-up appointment in 2-3 weeks with palliative care NP
- Social work referral placed for patient and family counseling support; connect with hospital-affiliated grief and support services
- Discussed chaplaincy services — patient expressed interest
- Lorazepam 0.5 mg orally q8h PRN anxiety — prescribing a small supply; patient and Claudia counseled on appropriate use
- Encouraged patient to continue engagement with church community for spiritual support

ADVANCE CARE PLANNING:
- POLST/MOLST form initiated and discussed in detail today. Patient clearly states he does not want CPR or mechanical ventilation in the event of cardiopulmonary arrest. He wishes to be DNR/DNI. He would accept hospitalization for comfort measures only (e.g., symptom management), not for aggressive intervention. Claudia is his healthcare proxy (documented previously). His adult daughter is aware and supportive of his wishes.
- Formal POLST document completed and signed by patient at today's visit. Copy provided to patient, copy placed in chart, copy given to Claudia for home file and to share with his PCP.
- Discussed hospice eligibility and the nature of hospice care — as a service that can provide expert comfort-focused care at home, with a dedicated care team. Patient expressed that when the time comes, he would want to be at home, not in a hospital or facility. We validated this clearly as a goal we will work to support.
- If he wishes to transition to hospice care in the future (with cessation of all systemic therapy), we will facilitate this. He understands this is a future option and is not being asked to make that decision today.

COMMUNICATION / FOLLOW-UP:
- Patient and family understand the overall plan and have my direct contact information
- Next oncology visit: 2-3 weeks (prior to next scheduled bevacizumab infusion)
- Next palliative care visit: 2-3 weeks with palliative care NP
- Labs at next visit: CBC, CMP, CEA, CA 19-9, iron studies, B12/folate
- CT restaging: 6-8 weeks
- Dietitian appointment: this week
- Social work: appointment being scheduled

Mr. Delgado and his family were thanked for their trust and courage in this difficult conversation. We reaffirmed our commitment to supporting him through whatever comes next, prioritizing his comfort and his time at home with those he loves.

Signed: [Attending Oncologist, MD], Gastrointestinal Oncology
Co-signed: [Palliative Care Attending, MD], Palliative Medicine
Note dictated and finalized at this visit.
